claims.claim: Rohingya community faces 12 hate speech incidents monthly
claims.source: No primary source found. Closest located source: Pusat KOMAS, Malaysia Racism Report 2024, published March 2025, https://komas.org/download/Malaysia-Racism-Report-2024.pdf
claims.correction: I could not verify any primary source recording 12 Rohingya-targeted hate speech incidents per month in Malaysia in 2024. The located Pusat KOMAS report documents 73 total racism, racial discrimination and xenophobia cases in 2024, not 144 Rohingya-targeted incidents.
claims.stale_check: No evidence this was correct at publication time; the analysis was derived on 2026-05-17, after the March 2025 Pusat KOMAS report.

claims.claim: Monitoring by human rights groups recorded an average of 12 hate speech incidents targeting Rohingya refugees monthly in Malaysia during 2024.
claims.source: No primary source found. Pusat KOMAS, Malaysia Racism Report 2024, lines 119-122, documents 73 total cases, https://komas.org/download/Malaysia-Racism-Report-2024.pdf
claims.correction: Replace with a sourced figure if available. Current verification supports only that Pusat KOMAS recorded 73 total racism, racial discrimination and xenophobia cases in Malaysia in 2024.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found that would make the 12-per-month claim current.

claims.claim: 144 documented hate speech incidents targeting Rohingya in Malaysia in 2024
claims.source: Pusat KOMAS, Malaysia Racism Report 2024, lines 119-122, https://komas.org/download/Malaysia-Racism-Report-2024.pdf
claims.correction: The located monitoring report records 73 total racism, racial discrimination and xenophobia cases in 2024. It does not record 144 Rohingya-specific hate speech incidents.
claims.stale_check: Not stale; contradicted by the available 2024 report.

claims.claim: Social media amplifies xenophobia against Southeast Asia's largest refugee group
claims.source: Pusat KOMAS, Malaysia Racism Report 2024 press statement, 21 March 2025, https://komas.org/pusat-komas-commemorates-the-international-day-for-the-elimination-of-racial-discrimination-with-the-launch-of-the-malaysia-racism-report-2024-and-a-special-10-year-review/; UNHCR Malaysia country page, crawled 2026-05-17, https://www.unhcr.org/my/countries/malaysia
claims.correction: The social-media xenophobia component is broadly supported by Pusat KOMAS, but I could not verify the wording that Rohingya are Southeast Asia's largest refugee group from a primary source. UNHCR states Malaysia hosts about 103,000 Rohingya, but the regional superlative needs sourcing.
claims.stale_check: Regional refugee figures change; this should be checked against current UNHCR regional data before publication.

claims.claim: No specific anti-hate speech legislation exists in Malaysian law
claims.source: Government of Malaysia response to OHCHR special procedures, paragraphs 21-22, https://spcommreports.ohchr.org/TMResultsBase/DownLoadFile?gId=35600
claims.correction: Malaysia may lack a dedicated standalone hate-speech statute, but the Government states hate speech and xenophobic remarks are dealt with under CMA sections 211 and 233, the Sedition Act section 4 read with section 3, and Penal Code section 505.
claims.stale_check: A 2025 amendment and 2026 Federal Court developments affected online speech enforcement; wording should be updated to avoid implying a total legal vacuum.

claims.claim: CMA and Penal Code rarely applied to protect minority communities from hate speech
claims.source: Government of Malaysia response to OHCHR special procedures, paragraphs 21-23, https://spcommreports.ohchr.org/TMResultsBase/DownLoadFile?gId=35600
claims.correction: I found official confirmation that CMA and Penal Code provisions may be used against hate speech and xenophobic remarks, but no primary enforcement dataset proving they are rarely applied to protect minority communities.
claims.stale_check: Enforcement patterns may have changed after 2025 amendments and 2026 court rulings.

claims.claim: 102,000 Rohingya registered with UNHCR Malaysia as of 2025
claims.source: UNHCR Malaysia country page, crawled 2026-05-17, https://www.unhcr.org/my/countries/malaysia; UNHCR Malaysia Annual Results Report 2024, May 28, 2025, https://www.unhcr.org/us/sites/en-us/files/2025-06/Malaysia%20ARR%202024_1.pdf
claims.correction: UNHCR's current Malaysia country page says about 103,000 Rohingya, while its 2024 Annual Results Report gives 192,000 total registered refugees and asylum-seekers and 89% from Myanmar without a Rohingya subtotal. State the date and source precisely.
claims.stale_check: Refugee registration figures change frequently. As of the current UNHCR country page, the figure is about 103,000, not exactly 102,000.

claims.claim: Without legal status, Rohingya cannot report crimes or seek legal protection
claims.source: UNHCR Refugee Malaysia, Legal Protection page, reporting a crime section, crawled 2026-05-17, https://refugeemalaysia.org/support/legal-protection/; UNHCR Malaysia Annual Results Report 2024, May 28, 2025
claims.correction: Refugees lack formal legal status and face barriers, arrest risk, detention risk, and limited access to justice, but UNHCR explicitly instructs refugees how to make police reports and seek legal aid. The claim should be changed to 'face serious barriers and risks when seeking legal protection.'
claims.stale_check: Not stale; current UNHCR refugee guidance contradicts the absolute claim.

claims.claim: Survey found 78% of Rohingya in KL experienced discrimination accessing services
claims.source: No primary source found after targeted searches for the exact figure. Related source: UNHCR Malaysia Annual Results Report 2024, May 28, 2025, discusses barriers to services but not this 78% KL survey figure.
claims.correction: Remove unless the survey can be cited directly with methodology, sample size, date, and exact wording.
claims.stale_check: No evidence found that a later survey made the figure current.

claims.claim: Hate speech translates to denial of healthcare, housing, and employment
claims.source: UNHCR Malaysia Annual Results Report 2024, May 28, 2025, lines 99-107 and 617-620, https://www.unhcr.org/us/sites/en-us/files/2025-06/Malaysia%20ARR%202024_1.pdf
claims.correction: UNHCR verifies barriers to healthcare, accommodation, and employment, but I did not find primary evidence that hate speech directly causes denial of those services. Reframe as correlation or broader hostile public discourse unless causation is sourced.

claims.claim: 12 incidents per month against people who cannot seek legal protection is systemic
claims.source: UNHCR Refugee Malaysia, Legal Protection page, https://refugeemalaysia.org/support/legal-protection/; Pusat KOMAS Malaysia Racism Report 2024, https://komas.org/download/Malaysia-Racism-Report-2024.pdf
claims.correction: Both premises are weak: the 12-per-month figure is unverified, and the claim that refugees cannot seek legal protection is contradicted by UNHCR guidance. A defensible version would say refugees face serious structural barriers to protection.
claims.stale_check: Not stale; the cited current sources contradict the wording.

claims.claim: Like being attacked 12 times a month in a building where you are not allowed to file a police report
claims.source: UNHCR Refugee Malaysia, Legal Protection page, lines 160-184, https://refugeemalaysia.org/support/legal-protection/
claims.correction: UNHCR states refugees can make police reports at police stations. The analogy falsely states they are not allowed to file reports.
claims.stale_check: Not stale; current UNHCR guidance contradicts this.

claims.claim: Refugees facing systematic hate speech cannot access legal protection because their legal status denies it
claims.source: UNHCR Malaysia Annual Results Report 2024, May 28, 2025, lines 49-52 and 401-410; UNHCR Refugee Malaysia Legal Protection page, https://refugeemalaysia.org/support/legal-protection/
claims.correction: Refugees face major barriers and risks due to lack of legal status, including arrest, detention and limited access to justice, but they are not categorically denied access to legal protection or police reporting.

claims.claim: Suhakam called for anti-discrimination legislation in its 2024 annual report
claims.source: Bernama, 24 July 2025, says SUHAKAM 2024 Annual Report was still being finalised, https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2449080; SUHAKAM Media Statement No. 20-2025, 3 May 2025, https://suhakam.org.my/2025/05/media-statement-no-20-2025_strengthening-racial-harmony-a-call-to-action-for-a-more-united-malaysia/
claims.correction: I verified SUHAKAM called for a National Harmony Act in a 3 May 2025 media statement, not in the 2024 annual report. The annual-report attribution should be removed unless the report text is supplied.
claims.stale_check: Possible later publication of the 2024 annual report should be checked, but the located evidence supports a 2025 media statement, not the annual report.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: 144 documented hate speech incidents targeting Rohingya in Malaysia in 2024
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No primary source found; the located Pusat KOMAS 2024 report records 73 total cases across racism, racial discrimination and xenophobia, not 144 Rohingya hate-speech incidents.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Without legal status, Rohingya cannot report crimes or seek legal protection
cardinal_sin_flags.why: UNHCR guidance explicitly explains how refugees can make police reports and seek legal aid. The evidence supports barriers and risks, not categorical inability.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: No specific anti-hate speech legislation exists in Malaysian law
cardinal_sin_flags.why: A standalone hate-speech law may be absent, but the Government says CMA, Sedition Act and Penal Code provisions can be used to address hate speech and xenophobic remarks.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Government of Malaysia response to OHCHR, https://spcommreports.ohchr.org/TMResultsBase/DownLoadFile?gId=35600

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Survey found 78% of Rohingya in KL experienced discrimination accessing services
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No survey or primary source for the exact 78% KL figure was located.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Suhakam called for anti-discrimination legislation in its 2024 annual report
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Verified SUHAKAM call was in a 3 May 2025 media statement calling for a National Harmony Act; the 2024 annual-report attribution was not verified.

omitted_facts: Pusat KOMAS' Malaysia Racism Report 2024 recorded 73 total cases of racism, racial discrimination and xenophobia, not 144 Rohingya-specific incidents: https://komas.org/download/Malaysia-Racism-Report-2024.pdf

UNHCR's Malaysia Annual Results Report 2024 says Malaysia had not established a national asylum framework in 2024, leaving refugees and asylum-seekers without legal status and vulnerable to arrest and detention: https://www.unhcr.org/us/sites/en-us/files/2025-06/Malaysia%20ARR%202024_1.pdf
UNHCR Refugee Malaysia guidance says refugees can make police reports and describes case reports and action reports: https://refugeemalaysia.org/support/legal-protection/

The Government of Malaysia told OHCHR that hate speech and xenophobic remarks may be dealt with under CMA sections 211 and 233, the Sedition Act, and Penal Code section 505: https://spcommreports.ohchr.org/TMResultsBase/DownLoadFile?gId=35600

SUHAKAM called for a National Harmony Act in a 3 May 2025 media statement after referring to the Pusat KOMAS Malaysia Racism Report 2024: https://suhakam.org.my/2025/05/media-statement-no-20-2025_strengthening-racial-harmony-a-call-to-action-for-a-more-united-malaysia/
source_assessment: The original analysis cites only two internal T4A related issues and no external primary sources, creating circular sourcing risk. The factual basis appears to depend on NGO monitoring, UNHCR figures, legal claims, and SUHAKAM statements, but these were not cited directly. Source range is extremely narrow and omits government legal sources, UNHCR protection guidance, primary legal text, Hansard, and the actual Pusat KOMAS report.
overall_assessment: The analysis is factually unreliable in its current form. The central 144 incidents / 12 per month claim could not be verified and appears inconsistent with the located Pusat KOMAS 2024 report. Several claims overstate the legal exclusion of refugees by saying they cannot file police reports or seek protection, which is contradicted by UNHCR guidance. The issue is real and serious, but the published cards need substantial correction, tighter sourcing, and removal of unsupported numbers.
